slides.title: Your Data, Your Rules
slides.body: **Encapsulation** means bundling data and the methods that use it into one unit — and deciding what the outside world is allowed to touch. Think of a vending machine: customers press buttons, they don't reach inside.
slides.tip: Encapsulation is one of the four pillars of object-oriented programming.

slides.title: Bundle Data With Methods
slides.body: A class already groups data and behavior together. Here a `BankAccount` keeps its `balance` alongside `deposit()` — they travel as one tidy package.

slides.title: Public, Protected, Private
slides.body: Python uses **naming conventions** to signal access level — no special keyword needed. One underscore means *"handle with care"*; two underscores triggers **name mangling** so outside access fails.
slides.code:
class Employee:
    def __init__(self, name, salary):
        self.name = name          # public
        self._department = "Eng"  # protected
        self.__salary = salary    # private

emp = Employee("Bob", 80000)
print(emp.name)
print(emp._department)
# emp.__salary  → AttributeError!
slides.tip: Python trusts you — underscores are a social contract, not a hard lock.

slides.title: Protect Rules With Methods
slides.body: Hiding data lets you **enforce invariants** — rules that must always hold. Route all changes through a method and validate there, in one place.
slides.tip: If `balance` were public, anyone could write `acc.balance = -999` and break your rule.

slides.title: Clean Access With @property
slides.body: The `@property` decorator lets you expose a private value **as if it were a plain attribute**, while secretly running validation or computation behind the scenes.
slides.tip: Callers write `t.celsius = 100` — they never know a method is running underneath.

slides.title: You've Got Encapsulation!
slides.body: You now know how to **bundle data with methods**, use `_` and `__` naming conventions for access control, and add `@property` for clean, validated attribute access. Your objects are safe, organized, and easy to use.
slides.tip: Start public, then add `@property` when you need validation — callers never notice the difference.
